volumeTitle: Stichus. Trinummus. Truculentus. Tale of a Travelling Bag. Fragments
label: Truculentus (Section 4.3.846 - Section 5.1.919)

sections.label: Section 4.3.846
sections.original: Bene agis mecum .
sections.translation: You act kindly towards me .

sections.label: Section 4.3.846b
sections.original: Filium istinc tuom te meliust repetere .
sections.translation: ’ Twere best for you to demand your son back from thence . ( Pointing to the house of PHRONESIUM .) But your wife , as soon as possible , take away from my house . I shall at once , therefore , send a messenger to that kinsman of mine by marriage , and tell him to look out for another match for his son .

sections.label: Section 4.3.850
sections.original: At ego ab hac puerum reposcam , ne mox infitias eat ;
sections.translation: But I ’ ll demand back the child of her , lest by-and-by she should deny it . That ’ s of no use ; for she herself , of her own accord , has discovered the whole matter to me , how it happened . But see , right opportunely , i ’ faith , is she coming out of doors from her house . Assuredly , a far-darting sting has that woman , who even from that distance is wounding my heart .

sections.label: Section 4.4.854
sections.original: Blitea et luteast meretrix nisi quae sapit in vino ad rem suam ;
sections.translation: A woman is a spoony and a trolloping slut , if she hasn ’ t a view to her own interests , even in her cups .

sections.label: Section 4.4.855
sections.original: si alia membra vino madeant , cor sit saltem sobrium .
sections.translation: If her other limbs are soaked in wine , at least let her head be sober . But it ’ s a vexation to me that my hair-dresser has been thus badly treated . She has been telling me that this child has been discovered to be the son of Dinarchus . When I heard that ⋯ ( She moves , as if going .)

sections.label: Section 4.4.858a
sections.original: Lubet adire quam penes est mea omnis res et liberi .
sections.translation: She ’ s going , in whose hands are all my fortune and my children .

sections.label: Section 4.4.859
sections.original: Video eccum qui suis tutorem med optavit liberis .
sections.translation: I see him who has constituted me the guardian of his property .

sections.label: Section 4.4.860
sections.original: Mulier , ad te sum profectus .
sections.translation: Madam , here am I .

sections.label: Section 4.4.860b
sections.original: Quid agitur , voluptas mea ?
sections.translation: It certainly is he . What ’ s the matter , my love ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.861
sections.original: Non voluptas , aufer nugas , nil ego nunc de istac re ago .
sections.translation: No love ; cease your trifling . I ’ ve nothing now to do with that subject .

sections.label: Section 4.4.862
sections.original: Scio mecastor quid velis ét quid postules et quid petas :
sections.translation: By my faith , I know what you want , and what you desire , and what you ask for . You want to see me ; you desire to caress me ; you ask for the child .

sections.label: Section 4.4.864
sections.original: Di immortales , ut planiloqua est , paucis ut rem ipsam attigit .
sections.translation: Immortal Gods ! how plain she speaks . How , in a few words , has she hit upon the very point !

sections.label: Section 4.4.865
sections.original: Scio equidem sponsam tibi esse et filium ex sponsa tua
sections.translation: As for me , I know that you are betrothed , and that you have a son by your betrothed , and that a wife is now going to be married by you ; that now your thoughts are elsewhere , that myself you are going to consider as forsaken . But still consider , the little mouse , how sagacious an animal it is , which never entrusts its life to one hole only ;

sections.label: Section 4.4.870
sections.original: quin , si unum obsideatur , aliud iam perfugium ele gerit .
sections.translation: inasmuch as , if one hole is blocked up , it seeks another as a place of refuge .

sections.label: Section 4.4.871
sections.original: Otium ubi erit , de istis rebus tum amplius tecum loquar .
sections.translation: When there ’ s leisure , then I ’ ll talk to you on those matters more at large ; at present , give me up the child .

sections.label: Section 4.4.872b
sections.original: Immo amabo ut hos dies aliquos sinas
sections.translation: No ; do , there ’ s a dear , let it be at my house the few next days .

sections.label: Section 4.4.873b
sections.original: Minime .
sections.translation: Certainly not .

sections.label: Section 4.4.873c
sections.original: Amabo .
sections.translation: Do , there ’ s a dear .

sections.label: Section 4.4.873d
sections.original: Quid opus est ?
sections.translation: What occasion is there ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.873e
sections.original: In rem meam est .
sections.translation: It ’ s for my interest . This for the next three days at least , until the Captain is circumvented somehow ;

sections.label: Section 4.4.875
sections.original: sine me habere : siquidem habebo , tibi quoque etiam proderit ;
sections.translation: for that same purpose . If I get anything , it shall be for your own advantage as well . If you take the child away , all hope in the Captain will evaporate from my heart .

sections.label: Section 4.4.877
sections.original: Factum cupio , nam nefacere si velim , non est locus ;
sections.translation: I would have that done ; but , when it ’ s taken home , to do it again [*] , if I were to wish it , I have not the opportunity . Now make use of the child , and take care of it , because you have the means by which to take care of it .

sections.label: Section 4.4.879
sections.original: Multum amo te ób istam rem mecastor . ubi domi metues malum ,
sections.translation: Upon my faith , I do love you much for this matter . When you shall be afraid of a scolding at home ,

sections.label: Section 4.4.880
sections.original: fugito huc ad me : saltem amicus mi esto † manubinarius .
sections.translation: do you take shelter here in my house . At least , prove a friend , to help me to a profitable speculation .

sections.label: Section 4.4.881
sections.original: Bene vale , Phronesium .
sections.translation: Kindly farewell , Phronesium .

sections.label: Section 4.4.881b
sections.original: An non et iam tuom oculum vocas ?
sections.translation: Won ’ t you any longer call me apple of your eye ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.882
sections.original: Id quoque interim † futatim nomen commemorabitur .
sections.translation: That name too , meanwhile , shall be repeated full oft .

sections.label: Section 4.4.883b
sections.original: Fac valeas .
sections.translation: Do you wish for anything else ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.883c
sections.original: Operae ubi mi erit , ad te venero .—
sections.translation: Fare thee well ; when I have leisure , I ’ ll come to your house .

sections.label: Section 4.4.884
sections.original: Ille quidem hinc abiit , abscessit . dicere hic quidvis licet .
sections.translation: Well , he ’ s gone away from here , and has taken his departure ; we may say here whatever we please .

sections.label: Section 4.4.885
sections.original: verum est verbum quod memoratur : ubi amici ibidem sunt opes .
sections.translation: ’ Tis a true proverb that ’ s quoted , Where the friends are , there are the riches . Through him , there ’ s still some hope that the Captain may be duped to-day ; whom , by the powers , I love better than my own self ,— so long as I get out of him what I want : since , when we have got much , not much of it is seen that has been given . Such are the brilliant prospects of Courtesans !

sections.label: Section 4.4.889b
sections.original: Áha tace .
sections.translation: Hush ! hush ! be quiet .

sections.label: Section 4.4.889c
sections.original: Quid est , obsecro ?
sections.translation: Prithee , what is it ?

sections.label: Section 4.4.890
sections.original: Pater adest pueri .
sections.translation: The father of the child is coming .

sections.label: Section 4.4.890b
sections.original: Sine eumpse adire huc . sine , si is est modo .
sections.translation: Well , let him come here . Let him , if it only is he ,

sections.label: Section 4.4.892
sections.original: Ne istum ecastor hodie aspiciam confectum fallaciis .
sections.translation: If he does come , for very sure , i ’ faith , I ’ ll do him to-day with some cunning tricks .

sections.label: Section 5.1.893
sections.original: Ego minam auri fero supplicium † damnis ad amicam meam :
sections.translation: That I should love [*] for this ! I ’ m taking an atonement for my offences to my mistress ! That that may be taken by her in kindly part which I ’ ve squandered before , I ’ ll add this as well .

sections.label: Section 5.1.895
sections.original: sed quid video ? eram atque ancillam ante aedis . adeundae haec mihi .
sections.translation: But what ’ s this ? I see the mistress and her maid before the house . I must accost her . ( Addressing them .) What are you doing here ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.896b
sections.original: Ne me appella quaeso .
sections.translation: Don ’ t speak to me .

sections.label: Section 5.1.896c
sections.original: Aha nimium saeviter .
sections.translation: You are too angry .

sections.label: Section 5.1.897
sections.original: Potine ut mihi molestus ne sis ?
sections.translation: Leave me alone . Can ’ t you possibly cease to be an annoyance to me ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.897b
sections.original: Ecquid , Astaphium , litiumst ?
sections.translation: What is the matter , my dear little Astaphium ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.898
sections.original: Merito ecastor tibi succenset .
sections.translation: I ’ faith , she ’ s angry with you with good reason .

sections.label: Section 5.1.898b
sections.original: Egon , atque isti etiam parum
sections.translation: What , I ? I ’ m not even half spiteful enough to . wards that fellow .

sections.label: Section 5.1.899b
sections.original: Ego , mea voluptas , si quid peccavi prius ,
sections.translation: My love , if I have at all offended before ,

sections.label: Section 5.1.900
sections.original: supplicium ad te hanc mínam fero auri . si minus crédis , respice .
sections.translation: I present you with this mina of gold . If you smile upon me , deign me a look .

sections.label: Section 5.1.901
sections.original: Manus vetat prius quám penes sese habeat quicquam credere .
sections.translation: My hand forbids me to believe anything , before it holds in its possession . We require food for the child ; we require it for the dame [*] as well , that bathes the child ; we require it for the nurse [*] , as well , that she may have a leather bottle full of old wine in ample style , that night and day she may tipple ; we stand in need of fire ; we want coals , too ;

sections.label: Section 5.1.905
sections.original: fasciis opus est , pulvinis , cunis , incunabulis ,
sections.translation: we want swathes , napkins , the cradle , the cradle-bed ; oil we want ; the child requires flour , for pap ; all day we are wanting something ; never , in the same one day , can our task be performed , but what there ’ s always need of something ; for the children of officers cannot be reared upon medlars [*] .

sections.label: Section 5.1.909
sections.original: Respice ergo , áccipe hoc , qui ístuc efficias opus .
sections.translation: Look upon me then . Take this ( presenting the money ) , with which to satisfy these necessities .

sections.label: Section 5.1.910
sections.original: Cedo , quamquam parum est .
sections.translation: Give it me , although it ’ s very little .

sections.label: Section 5.1.911
sections.original: Tuo arbitratu quod iubebis dabitur . da nunc savium .
sections.translation: Whatever you shall order , shall be given at your demand . Give me a kiss now . ( Tries to kiss her .)

sections.label: Section 5.1.912
sections.original: Mitte me , inquam , odiosu'sodiosus es .
sections.translation: Leave me alone , I say ! You are a nuisance !

sections.label: Section 5.1.912b
sections.original: Nil fit , non amor , teritur dies .
sections.translation: It ’ s no use , I ’ m not loved by her ; the day wears apace . More than ten pounds of silver have I lost in this short time by reason of my passion .

sections.label: Section 5.1.914
sections.original: Accipe hoc atque auferto intro .
sections.translation: Take this , and carry it away in-doors . ( ASTAPHIUM carries it in .) ( Enter STRABAX , from the house .)

sections.label: Section 5.1.914b
sections.original: Vbi mea amicast gentium ?
sections.translation: Where in the world is my mistress ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.915
sections.original: neque ruri neque hic óperis quicquam facio , corrumpor situ ,
sections.translation: I get on with no business , either in the country or here , at this rate ; I ’ m spoiling with mouldiness , I ’ m grown so dreadfully numbed with lying waiting here upon the couch . But look , I perceive her . Hallo ! sweetheart , what are you about ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.917b
sections.original: Quis illic est homo ?
sections.translation: What fellow is that ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.918
sections.original: Quém ego ecastor mage amo quam te . ⋯
sections.translation: One that , upon my honor , I love far more than yourself .

sections.label: Section 5.1.918b
sections.original: Quam me ? quo modo ?
sections.translation: Than myself ? In what way ?

sections.label: Section 5.1.919
sections.original: Hoc modo , ut molestus ne sis .
sections.translation: Why , this way , that you are not to be troublesome to me . ( Moves as if going .)
